title: SEGMENT 6 — Five flavors of antibody and why we need them

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Five flavors of antibody and why we need them
We've been talking, so far, about antibodies as if they were all one thing.
They're not.
There are five major classes of antibodies in humans — five flavors, with five different jobs — and they share the same Fab arms (in any given B cell) but they differ in the Fc handle at the bottom.
Different Fc, different downstream function.
The five classes are called IgG, IgM, IgA, IgE, and IgD.
"Ig" stands for immunoglobulin, which is just the formal name for antibody.
The single letter at the end — G, M, A, E, D — names the class, also called the isotype.
Here is the analogy I want to use, because I think it makes the whole thing intuitive.
Think of the Fab arm — the gripping hand — as the arrow tip.
It's what hits the target; it's what does the recognition.
But the same arrow tip can be mounted on different shafts, with different fletching, for different kinds of shooting.
The shaft and fletching don't change what the arrow hits — that's the tip's job.
But they change how the arrow flies, where it can go, how stable it is, what kind of bowstring it pairs with.
The Fc region is the shaft.
Different Fc, different shaft, different kind of arrow for different combat situations.
Let me take you through them.
IgM — this is the first antibody class your B cells make when they encounter a new threat.
Before any affinity maturation, before any class switching, the default is IgM.

And IgM has a remarkable structural feature: it floats around in your blood as a pentamer — five Y-shaped units linked together by their Fc tails into a star shape, giving it ten antigen-binding arms total.
Why?
Because IgM antibodies, fresh out of the warehouse, haven't been refined yet — individually they're often low-affinity.
The ten arms compensate by maximizing avidity.
Even a weak grip, multiplied by ten simultaneous handshakes, becomes a strong overall hold.
IgM is also exceptionally good at activating the complement cascade, that hole-punching system in your blood.
So IgM is the first-volley arrow — fletched for early shots, when you don't yet have a refined match, but you need to throw something at the threat fast and stick to it any way you can.
IgG — this is the workhorse.
By far the most abundant antibody class in your blood — about seventy-five percent of the antibody in your bloodstream is IgG.
IgG appears later in an immune response, after affinity maturation has refined the Fab arms in the germinal center.
Its Fc is recognized by basically every immune cell type and by the complement system.

It's also small enough and stable enough to cross from a mother's bloodstream into a developing fetus through the placenta, which is why newborn babies arrive with their mother's IgG repertoire pre-installed — temporary borrowed immunity that lasts a few months while the baby's own immune system gets going.
IgG is the sustained-fire arrow — fletched for long campaigns, deployed in massive quantity, recognized by everything in the immune system, and durable enough to be passed across generations.
Almost every therapeutic antibody on the market today is an IgG, for these reasons.
IgA — this is the antibody class that defends your mucous membranes.
Mouth, nose, gut, lungs, urinary tract — anywhere your body has a wet, exposed surface in contact with the outside world.
IgA is secreted across epithelial cells into mucus, where it bathes the surface and grabs incoming pathogens before they can attach to cells.
It's also the antibody in breast milk — a nursing mother coats her baby's gut with her own IgA, providing direct frontline protection.

Structurally, secreted IgA is usually a dimer — two Y units linked together — with an extra piece called a secretory component that helps protect it from being digested by enzymes in mucus and gut fluid.
IgA is the mucosal-wall arrow — fletched specifically for the wet borderlands of the body, tough enough to survive in hostile mucosal environments where most proteins would get chewed up.
IgE — this is the strangest of the five.
It's present in very low concentrations in blood, and it doesn't really float around freely.
Instead, IgE binds tightly to receptors on the surface of two cell types called mast cells and basophils, decorating those cells with antigen-recognition arms.
When the IgE on a mast cell encounters its target, the cell explodes — well, degranulates — releasing histamine and a flood of inflammatory mediators.

In evolutionary context, this is your anti-parasite system; IgE evolved to deal with worms and large parasites, where you needed a dramatic, inflammatory, localized response to make life unpleasant for the invader.
In modern industrialized environments, where we have very few parasites, IgE has become better known as the cause of allergies.
Hay fever, food allergies, asthma — these are IgE responses to harmless targets that the immune system has mistakenly tagged as threats.
The mechanism is identical to anti-parasite defense; just the target is wrong.
IgE is the parasite-and-allergy arrow — fletched for explosive, inflammatory response.
Use sparingly.
IgD — and we know least about this one, honestly.
It's expressed on the surface of immature B cells, where along with surface IgM it serves as a B cell receptor — the signal-receiving form of an antibody before the cell has been activated.
There's a little IgD in serum and some interesting recent biology about it in the upper airway, but for our purposes today, IgD's main job is as a marker on naive B cells.
You can mostly file it under "exists, and we're still figuring it out.
Now — here's the mechanism by which a single B cell ends up making different isotypes.
Remember, the B cell's particular Fab arms are determined by its V(D)J rearrangement, and once that's done, it's done.
But the Fc region the cell uses can be switched.

After a B cell has been activated, in the germinal center, it can undergo a process called class switching — also called isotype switching — in which it physically deletes a chunk of its DNA that contains one Fc-coding region and joins its Fab-coding region to a different Fc-coding region downstream.
The new heavy chain is made with the same V(D)J — same Fab — but with a new constant region, a new Fc, a new flavor.
So a single B cell typically starts out making IgM.
Then, depending on what kind of infection it sees and what cytokine signals it receives from helper T cells, it can switch to making IgG, or IgA, or IgE — same Fab, different Fc.
Same arrow tip, different shaft.

This is why a mature antibody response includes multiple isotypes against the same target: IgG circulating in the blood, IgA secreted into mucus, IgE on mast cells in tissue — all of them with identical Fab specificity for the pathogen, but distributed to different body compartments and recruiting different downstream effectors, by virtue of their different Fc handles.

The choice of which isotype to switch to is regulated by cytokines from T helper cells — TH1 cytokines push toward IgG subclasses good at intracellular pathogens, TH2 cytokines push toward IgE and certain IgGs good at parasites and extracellular threats.
We don't need to memorize the wiring today.
The big idea is: one B cell, one Fab, but flexibility in choosing which Fc to attach to it, and therefore flexibility in what kind of downstream response the antibody can recruit.
That's the elegance of the class-switching system.
Alright.
We've got the molecule, the diversity, the refinement, the binding, and the flavors.
Now let's spend a little time on the cells that make all this.
Then we'll break.
